claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 8 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First of all, could you please tell me how many children you would like to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to claim Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to know about Alex.

Now, could you please tell me - do you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. That's helpful to know.

Could you tell me about your living arrangements with Alex? Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

Just a couple more questions to ensure we cover everything:

Is anyone else currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is receiving Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One final question: Is Alex in any of the following situations?

- In hospital
- In local authority care
- In residential accommodation paid for by the local council

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in local authority care for a little while now, but they still come home to stay with me regularly.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know about Alex's situation. That's helpful information.

Could you tell me how long Alex has been in local authority care? And when Alex comes home to stay with you, roughly how much time do they spend with you each week - for example, how many hours or nights per week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Alex has been in care for about 8 weeks now. When they come home, they spend quite a bit of time with me - around 24 hours a week, usually staying over on weekends.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing those details. That's very helpful.

Since Alex has been in care for about 8 weeks, this is right at the point where Child Benefit payments can be affected. However, there are exceptions to the rule.

You mentioned Alex spends around 24 hours a week at home with you. To clarify one more thing: Does Alex typically spend at least 24 hours a week at your home, or does this vary week to week?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex regularly spends at least 24 hours a week at home with me. It's pretty consistent - they come home every weekend.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that. This is really helpful information.

Based on what you've told me, I now have enough information to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex. Let me provide you with my assessment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's a summary of my assessment:

**For Alex (aged 8):** ✅ Eligible

Although Alex has been in local authority care for about 8 weeks, you still qualify for Child Benefit because:

1. You live in the UK
2. Alex is under 16 years old
3. Alex regularly spends at least 24 hours a week at home with you (this is an important exception to the usual rule that Child Benefit stops after 8 weeks in care)
4. No one else is currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex

**Important:** You should inform HM Revenue and Customs (HMRC) about Alex's care situation if you haven't already done so. This ensures your claim is properly recorded and helps avoid any issues with your payments.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and the claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex, who normally lives with them. Although Alex has been in local authority care for about 8 weeks (which is normally the point at which Child Benefit eligibility could be affected), there is an exception that applies in this case. The rules state that for children in care, the parent can continue to receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. The claimant has confirmed that Alex regularly and consistently spends at least 24 hours a week at home with them, staying over on weekends. Additionally, no one else is currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex (aged 8), who normally lives with them but has been in local authority care for about 8 weeks. Despite Alex being in care, the claimant qualifies for the exception that allows continued Child Benefit payments because Alex regularly spends at least 24 hours a week at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 8 years old (under 16), and the claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for Alex, who normally lives with them. Although Alex has been in local authority care for about 8 weeks (which is normally the point at which Child Benefit eligibility could be affected), there is an exception that applies in this case. The rules state that for children in care, the parent can continue to receive Child Benefit if the child spends at least 24 hours a week at home. The claimant has confirmed that Alex regularly and consistently spends at least 24 hours a week at home with them, staying over on weekends. Additionally, no one else is currently receiving Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, the claimant meets the eligibility criteria for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for one child, Alex (aged 8), who normally lives with them but has been in local authority care for about 8 weeks. Despite Alex being in care, the claimant qualifies for the exception that allows continued Child Benefit payments because Alex regularly spends at least 24 hours a week at home. No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.
